whatItDoes: Converts any string into a URL-friendly slug in real-time. Options include lowercasing, replacing spaces with hyphens, removing special characters, and truncating to a maximum length. The slug updates instantly as you type or change options.
whyItExists: Generating clean URL slugs from titles or arbitrary text is a common need in web development. Manual slug creation is error-prone and inconsistent, especially with special characters and non-English text.
whoShouldUse: Blog authors creating URL slugs from post titles, CMS developers building slug generators, SEO specialists creating clean URLs, and any developer who needs URL-safe identifiers.
useCases: Generate a URL slug from a blog post title like 'Hello World! My First Post'
Create clean URL-friendly identifiers from user-generated content
Truncate long titles into short, SEO-friendly slugs with a max length
Remove special characters and spaces to create machine-readable identifiers
instructions: Type or paste any text into the input field
Toggle options: lowercase, spaces to hyphens, remove special chars
Set a max length of 0 for no truncation, or specify a character limit
The slug updates in real-time as you type or change options
Click Copy to copy the slug to your clipboard
examples: "Hello World! This is a Post #1..." → hello-world-this-is-a-post-1
bestPractices: Keep lowercase enabled for consistent, URL-friendly slugs
Use truncation for titles that may be very long to keep URLs manageable
Test with special characters, accented letters, and numbers to ensure expected output
Consider keeping hyphens in the output for readability in URLs
commonMistakes: Setting max length too short, cutting off meaningful words
Forgetting to remove special characters that cause issues in URLs
Using the slug for file names without considering OS-specific character restrictions
faq: Does this handle Unicode or accented characters? — Accented characters are preserved unless Remove Special Chars is enabled, which may strip them.
Can I use this for file names? — Yes, but be aware some file systems have additional restrictions beyond URL safety.
What happens with multiple consecutive spaces or hyphens? — They are collapsed into a single hyphen automatically.
